novelty_findings: No prior nomination as an immune/T-cell drug target. PubMed: 0 hits for CLCC1 as a drug/therapeutic target and 0 in autoimmune/tumor-immunity context; the only 2 'immune' co-mentions are incidental GWAS/expression-panel hits (PMID 24080485, 23741398), not mechanistic. Its characterized biology is entirely non-immune: an ER anion/chloride channel governing ER ion & lipid homeostasis, implicated in ALS-like neurodegeneration and retinitis pigmentosa (PMID 40307212-adjacent CLCC1 papers e.g. 'CLCC1 governs ER bilayer equilibration', total 26 CLCC1 papers, none T-cell). No known drugs (Open Targets drugAndClinicalCandidates count=0; ChEMBL: no human target record). Druggability caveat: Open Targets tractability shows NO small-molecule pocket (only antibody-membrane and PROTAC/ubiquitination buckets) and it is a broadly-expressed essential ER housekeeping channel, so selectivity/toxicity is a concern. Not named in the Marson 2025 abstract. Verdict: genuinely new as an immune target, but undrugged and low small-molecule tractability.
druggability_findings: Chloride-channel-like protein confirmed — UniProt Q96S66 'Chloride channel MCLC family', keywords Chloride channel / Ion channel / Transmembrane, ER-localised with transmembrane helices; InterPro assigns only the single 'Chloride channel CLIC-like' family. Ion channels are a druggable superfamily in principle (small-molecule modulators), but for THIS member there is no chemical matter: no ChEMBL target/ligands, no experimental PDB structure (0 entries), and AlphaFold Q96S66 is low-confidence (pLDDT 60). Open Targets shows NO small-molecule tractability bucket (only an antibody signal-peptide/TMHMM flag and protein-level signals), and Open Targets flagged a safety liability event. The channel class hints at long-term small-molecule potential, but nothing is actionable today, and the ER-membrane localisation makes an antibody impractical. Catch: undrugged, unstructured, poorly characterised — any campaign starts from zero.
genetics_findings: Autoimmune direction (inhibitor should dampen immunity). No autoimmune GWAS association maps to CLCC1: all 7 GWAS Catalog gene-mapped hits are non-immune and sit in a shared GPSM2/CLCC1/AKNAD1 locus block at 1p13.3 (LDL/apoB p=6e-10 rs541155877; cadherin-EGF-LAG blood level p=4e-18 rs149700491; gut microbiome; oligodendroglioma; major depression) — none immune-mediated. FinnGen R12 region PheWAS returns an autoimmune-hypothyroidism hit (rs547761798, p=3.2e-10), but I verified this variant is NOT in GWAS Catalog (gwas_get_variant returned found=false), so no Ensembl gene mapping exists for it; its position chr1:109,039,207 lies ~76 kb beyond the CLCC1 3' end (gene body 108,929,508-108,963,457, minus strand), i.e. it is a region-padded FinnGen hit outside the CLCC1 gene body and cannot be attributed to CLCC1 on position. gnomAD r4 constraint: pLI~0, LOEUF 0.81 (LoF-tolerant, favourable for an inhibitor). ClinGen: no gene-disease validity curation. OMIM/OT Mendelian links are neuro-sensory (Chudley-McCullough, retinitis pigmentosa 32, recessive deafness), not immune. No whole-blood eGene in GTEx v8 (0 significant cis-eQTLs). VERDICT NEUTRAL: no human-genetics evidence that reduced CLCC1 protects from or dampens autoimmune disease; none contradicting.

novelty_findings: Well-established T-cell signaling kinase, not novel. MAP3K1 (MEKK1) is a documented regulator of T-lymphocyte development/effector responses: T-cell-specific deletion (Lck-Cre) alters iNKT expansion and CDKN1B-dependent proliferation via MEKK1/JNK (PMID 26774476, Cell Rep 2016). PubMed: 57 immune co-mentions, 34 as inhibitor/drug target. It is druggable and already has a clinical-stage inhibitor: E-6201 (ChEMBL1097999), a small-molecule MAP3K1/MEK inhibitor at Phase 2 (Open Targets drugAndClinicalCandidates count=1; ChEMBL mechanism 'MAP3K1 inhibitor'). Open Targets tractability = 'SM: Advanced Clinical / High-Quality Ligand / Druggable Family'. Verdict: recovered-known-broad — a known, druggable kinase in the TCR/JNK activation pathway.
druggability_findings: Ser/Thr kinase MEKK1 confirmed — UniProt Q13233 'Protein kinase superfamily, STE, MAP kinase kinase kinase subfamily' (EC 2.7.11.25), InterPro protein-kinase domain plus RING/SWIM zinc fingers and a TOG domain. Best-characterised of the six for small molecules: Open Targets small-molecule tractability fires Advanced Clinical + High-Quality Ligand + Druggable Family. ChEMBL target CHEMBL3956 carries 53 potent (pChEMBL>=5) bioactivities, the most potent an 8 nM IC50 (CHEMBL318804); clinical candidate E-6201 (CHEMBL1097999, max_phase 2, dual MEK/MEKK1 inhibitor) has a curated MEKK1-inhibitor mechanism. Structural support: PDB 6WHB (kinase domain) plus AlphaFold Q13233 (though global pLDDT 59, the catalytic domain region is the ordered part). ATP-competitive small molecule is the natural modality. Catch: kinome-wide selectivity — E-6201 is a dual MEK/MEKK1 agent, so a MEKK1-selective inhibitor is not yet demonstrated, and this is a large 1512-aa multidomain scaffold.
genetics_findings: Autoimmune direction (inhibitor should dampen immunity). MAP3K1 (MEKK1) is a bona fide immune/inflammatory GWAS locus: psoriasis p=9e-12 (rs12654176, PMID 40021644 — note variant is intergenic, mapped to MAP3K1/C5orf67); FinnGen R12 region PheWAS reaches Crohn's disease of large intestine p=6.6e-6 (rs35512566), Graves ophthalmopathy p=5.0e-6, palindromic rheumatism p=1.8e-6. Open Targets top disease links are LoF-driven Mendelian 46,XY sex reversal/gonadal dysgenesis (genetic 0.90/0.61) and breast/prostate cancer. gnomAD r4: pLI~1.0, LOEUF 0.33 — strongly LoF-INTOLERANT, and complete LoF produces a Mendelian disorders-of-sex-development phenotype, so full knockout is not tolerated (partial inhibition only). Direction: locus associates with autoimmune/inflammatory disease and MAP3K1 sits upstream of TCR-driven JNK/NF-kB, consistent in principle with reduced activity dampening activation, but allele-direction-vs-expression colocalization was not established here, so consistency is by locus involvement rather than proven protective LoF alleles. VERDICT SUPPORTS (moderate): genuine autoimmune-locus genetics consistent with an immune target; flag LoF-intolerance + sex-reversal Mendelian phenotype as a dose/safety constraint.  [VERIFICATION (live GWAS Catalog gene mapping): psoriasis rs12654176 p=9e-12 PMID 40021644 CONFIRMED; polyarteritis nodosa rs535102440 p=2e-12 PMID 39024449 CONFIRMED (both genome-wide, mapped to MAP3K1/C5orf67). Crohn's/Graves' are FinnGen PheWAS signals at SUB-genome-wide significance (p~1e-6), not GWAS Catalog genome-wide loci — treat as suggestive, not established.]

novelty_findings: Canonical T-cell regulator, not novel. WAS encodes WASP (Wiskott-Aldrich Syndrome protein), a hematopoietic actin-nucleation regulator essential for immune-synapse formation and T-cell activation; loss-of-function causes Wiskott-Aldrich immunodeficiency. PubMed: ~1402 WASP/Wiskott-Aldrich + immune papers and ~759 in a therapeutic/gene-therapy context. Direction is consistent with the screen (knockdown lowers activation; germline LoF causes immunodeficiency), but that same LoF phenotype is a serious safety liability (combined immunodeficiency, thrombocytopenia). No small-molecule inhibitor drug exists (Open Targets drugAndClinicalCandidates count=0); the clinical modality is gene therapy to RESTORE function (opposite direction to an inhibitor). Verdict: recovered-known-broad — a foundational T-cell gene; an inhibitor would recapitulate a known immunodeficiency syndrome.
druggability_findings: Actin-nucleation-promoting scaffold (WASP) confirmed — UniProt P42768, no catalytic activity/family assignment, InterPro WH1/EVH1, CRIB and WH2 domains: a modular protein-protein / GTPase-interaction scaffold that activates the Arp2/3 complex. No enzyme active site to target. ChEMBL has no human target record with potent ligands; Open Targets small-molecule tractability shows only 'Structure with Ligand' (the PDB entries are peptide/GTPase-complex structures, not drug-like ligands), with antibody buckets from localisation only. PDB has 6 entries (1CEE, 1EJ5, 1T84, 2A3Z, 2K42, 2OT0) — isolated domains and complexes, several by NMR — and AlphaFold P42768 is moderate (pLDDT 69). Small molecule against a PPI scaffold is a hard modality; a degrader or oligo knockdown is more realistic mechanistically. Catch (safety, not just tractability): WAS has a ClinGen Definitive gene-disease validity classification for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (MONDO:0010518), and UniProt annotates it as the cause of Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome (MIM:301000, an X-linked immunodeficiency with thrombocytopenia and recurrent infection) as well as X-linked thrombocytopenia and X-linked severe congenital neutropenia — all loss-of-function phenotypes. A WASP inhibitor mimicking the knockdown therefore has a serious on-target immunodeficiency safety ceiling.
genetics_findings: Autoimmune direction (inhibitor should dampen immunity). WAS is X-linked; ClinGen curates DEFINITIVE gene-disease validity for Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome AND X-linked severe congenital neutropenia (OT genetic association 0.93 WAS, 0.86 XLN, 0.88 X-linked thrombocytopenia). gnomAD r4: pLI~1.0, LOEUF 0.12 — among the most LoF-intolerant genes. Critically, loss/reduction of WAS function does NOT produce clean immunosuppression: WAS-deficiency causes combined immunodeficiency WITH prominent autoimmunity and immune dysregulation (eczema, autoimmune cytopenias, IBD-like colitis, vasculitis in the human Mendelian syndrome), plus thrombocytopenia. No common-variant autoimmune GWAS maps to WAS (only a platelet-distribution-width hit rs146220228 p7e-9, PMID 32888494); FinnGen gene PheWAS returned zero significant immune endpoints. VERDICT CONTRADICTS: reduced WAS function drives immunodeficiency + autoimmunity rather than the intended dampening of the effector program, and the X-linked, extremely LoF-intolerant Mendelian context is a hard safety/druggability barrier for an inhibitor.

novelty_findings: No prior immune/target literature. PubMed: only 3 total RSBN1L papers, all incidental (CNS lymphoma genomic-alteration list, a sepsis circRNA panel, a cattle-pelvimetry SNP study) — none establishes a T-cell or immune-regulatory function, 0 as a drug target. Annotated as Round Spermatid Basic protein 1-Like; the RSBN1 family carries putative histone H4K20me demethylase (chromatin-modifying) activity, so a plausible transcriptional-brake mechanism, but this has not been described in T cells. No known drugs (Open Targets count=0; no ChEMBL human target). Not named in the Marson 2025 abstract. This was the highest-reproducibility brake hit in the screen (guide-reproducibility 1.000). Druggability caveat: Open Targets tractability shows NO small-molecule pocket (PROTAC/ubiquitination bucket only). Verdict: genuinely new — the strongest truly-novel brake candidate by reproducibility, but with an unproven mechanism and uncertain small-molecule tractability.
druggability_findings: Prior 'JmjC/round-spermatid demethylase' is partly right: UniProt Q6PCB5 'Round spermatid basic protein 1 family', annotated as an oxidoreductase/dioxygenase with Fe binding (EC 1.14.11.-, i.e. an Fe(II)/2-oxoglutarate dioxygenase-type enzyme, the KDM/2-OG-oxygenase superfamily), nuclear. InterPro gives only the RSBN1/Dpy-21/CEP530 family with no resolved catalytic-domain entry. This enzyme CLASS is druggable in principle (2-OG oxygenases have small-molecule inhibitors), but for this member there is no chemical matter whatsoever: no ChEMBL target/ligands, no PDB structure (0 entries), AlphaFold Q6PCB5 low-confidence (pLDDT 62), and Open Targets shows NO small-molecule tractability bucket (only protein-level ubiquitination/half-life). Small molecule is the sensible eventual modality given the enzyme class, but it would be a de novo campaign. Catch: the catalytic mechanism is unconfirmed at structure level and there is no ligand or structure to start from.
genetics_findings: Anti-tumor direction (gene is a brake; inhibitor should heighten immunity — look for reduced-function alleles associating with heightened/dysregulated immunity). RSBN1L at 7q11.23 shows the cleanest brake-loss genetic signature of the six: autoimmune hypothyroidism GWAS p=2e-24 (rs17385959/rs17385641, PMIDs 30595370/41644669) — variant is an intron of RSBN1L, mapped by GWAS Catalog to RSBN1L; eosinophil count p=2e-13 (rs3764824, a synonymous RSBN1L variant, PMID 32888494) and eosinophil % of leukocytes p=3e-10; TSH level p=4e-15. FinnGen R12 region PheWAS reaches SLE p=1.1e-7 (rs186122660), autoimmune hypothyroidism p=1.3e-6, asthma-and-allergy p=1.5e-6, rheumatic-disease reimbursement p=4e-7. Open Targets: hypothyroidism overall 0.349, genetic_association 0.574 — the top-scoring RSBN1L disease link. gnomAD r4: pLI 0.024, LOEUF 0.60 (LoF-tolerant, favourable for an inhibitor). Direction: multiple RSBN1L-body variants associate with autoimmune thyroid disease, SLE, eosinophilia and allergy — i.e. heightened/dysregulated adaptive immunity — matching a brake whose reduced function releases immune activation. VERDICT SUPPORTS. Caveat: allele-direction/eQTL colocalization to RSBN1L expression was not confirmed (RSBN1L is only a weak whole-blood eGene, 1 GTEx v8 cis-eQTL), so causal-gene attribution at the locus is supported by variant position but not locked by coloc.

novelty_findings: Direction already validated in the literature — not novel as an anti-tumor-immunity target. A 2025 Nature Communications paper explicitly shows 'Inhibition of MBTPS1 enhances antitumor immunity and potentiates anti-PD-1 immunotherapy' via an in vivo CRISPR screen in CRC models (PMID 40307212, DOI 10.1038/s41467-025-59193-4) — the same brake-release direction we nominate. Note that study is tumor-cell-intrinsic MBTPS1, whereas our screen implicates a T-cell-intrinsic effect (a partial-novelty nuance). MBTPS1 (site-1 protease / SKI-1, the SCAP-SREBP maturation protease) is druggable: potent SCAP/S1P-pathway degraders and inhibitors are published (e.g. PMID 32432943 SCAP degrader) and Open Targets flags 'SM: High-Quality Ligand / Druggable Family / Small Molecule Binder' (ChEMBL target CHEMBL5916). Open Targets drug count=0 (no approved/clinical drug named specifically to MBTPS1). Verdict: recovered-known-broad — the target-plus-direction is already published; residual novelty is only the T-cell-intrinsic angle.
druggability_findings: Subtilisin-like serine protease (Site-1 protease / S1P) confirmed — UniProt Q14703 'Peptidase S8 family' (EC 3.4.21.112), InterPro Peptidase S8/S53 catalytic domain plus the S1P-specific N-terminal and accessory domains. Open Targets small-molecule tractability fires High-Quality Ligand + Druggable Family (and a Small Molecule Binder protein-degrader signal). ChEMBL target CHEMBL5916 has 21 potent bioactivities with IC50s down to 140-170 nM (e.g. CHEMBL233609/233610/233611 aminopyrrolidine-type S1P inhibitors, consistent with the known PF-429242 chemotype). Recent experimental structures exist (PDB 8UW8, 8UWC, 9CSD) and AlphaFold Q14703 is high-confidence (pLDDT 81). Active-site-directed small-molecule inhibitor is the natural modality. Catch: S1P is an essential housekeeping protease (SREBP and ATF6 processing, cholesterol/ER-stress biology) — systemic inhibition carries broad on-target liability, which is a concern for the brake-release (anti-tumor) direction where you would be inhibiting it in the periphery.
genetics_findings: Anti-tumor direction (brake; look for reduced-function alleles heightening immunity). MBTPS1 (S1P/site-1 protease, SREBP+ATF6 processing) is strongly regulated in blood — 231 significant whole-blood cis-eQTLs in GTEx v8 — so it is genetically modulable in immune tissue, but there is NO direction-consistent immune genetics. No autoimmune GWAS maps to MBTPS1; Open Targets immune links are trivial (vitiligo overall 0.073, genetic_association 0.0; diffuse large B-cell lymphoma 0.087, genetic 0.0). FinnGen R12 region hits are non-immune (iron-deficiency anaemia p6.7e-7, low-back pain, idiopathic gout). ClinGen: DEFINITIVE for spondyloepiphyseal dysplasia (Kondo-Fu type) — a skeletal, not immune, Mendelian phenotype (OT genetic 0.84). gnomAD r4: pLI~0, LOEUF 0.87 (LoF-tolerant). VERDICT NEUTRAL: expressed and eQTL-active in blood but no human-genetics evidence that reduced MBTPS1 heightens immunity, so the brake-loss/inhibitor rationale is neither supported nor contradicted by genetics.

novelty_findings: Published as an immune-evasion / antitumor-immunity suppressor, but only in tumor-cell-intrinsic contexts and never nominated as a T-cell-intrinsic drug target. PUM1 (Pumilio-1, an RNA-binding translational repressor) suppresses antitumor immunity: it is DNA-methylation-upregulated to suppress antitumor immunity in pancreatic cancer (PMID 35116535) and drives PD-L1-mediated immune escape via an NPM3/NPM1 axis in gastric cancer (PMID 38029539). PubMed: 12 immune, 8 tumor-immunity co-mentions. These are tumor-cell-intrinsic; the brake direction (knockdown raises activation) is consistent, but a T-cell-intrinsic regulatory role is not previously described. No drug (Open Targets count=0; ChEMBL target CHEMBL6067257 has no mechanism/drug) and RNA-binding proteins are historically hard to drug (no SM tractability flagged). Not named in the Marson 2025 abstract. Verdict: partially-characterized — a known negative regulator of antitumor immunity in cancer cells, direction-consistent, but the T-cell-intrinsic brake role is novel and it is currently undrugged.
druggability_findings: RNA-binding protein confirmed — UniProt Q14671, no enzyme class, InterPro Pumilio-homology domain (PUF repeats, RNA-binding). Structurally very well characterised (15 PDB entries incl. 1M8W/1M8X/1M8Y and RNA complexes 3Q0L-3Q0P) but the ligand surface is an extended, curved single-stranded-RNA-binding groove — a classic hard PPI/RNA-interface target, not a compact pocket. Open Targets shows NO small-molecule tractability bucket (only protein-level ubiquitination/half-life signals); ChEMBL has a target record (CHEMBL6067257) but zero bioactivities/ligands. AlphaFold Q14671 is low-confidence overall (pLDDT 53; much of the protein is disordered outside the PUF repeats). Small molecule is not currently credible. Plausible modalities are an RNA/oligo knockdown to mimic the CRISPRi effect, or a targeted degrader (the ubiquitination tractability signal is the only chemical-biology handle). Catch: no chemical matter and an undruggable-by-small-molecule binding surface.
genetics_findings: Anti-tumor direction (brake; look for reduced-function alleles heightening immunity). No direction-consistent immune genetics for PUM1. Open Targets top links are neuro Mendelian: spinocerebellar ataxia 47 (genetic 0.84) and PUM1-related neurodevelopmental disorder with motor/developmental abnormality (genetic 0.77) — both HAPLOINSUFFICIENCY/reduced-dosage phenotypes (PADDAS/PRCA). FinnGen R12 region hits (acquired haemolytic anaemia p8.3e-8 rs1040826069, allergic urticaria p1.6e-6) are rare-variant, region-padded and not corroborated by GWAS Catalog gene mapping (PUM1's 21 GWAS-mapped hits carry no autoimmune trait). gnomAD r4: pLI 1.0, LOEUF 0.16 — very LoF-INTOLERANT. VERDICT NEUTRAL for immune direction, with a STRONG safety flag: because PUM1 haploinsufficiency already causes human neurodevelopmental/ataxia disease, a systemic inhibitor mimicking reduced PUM1 dosage carries serious on-target CNS liability — this argues against the target independent of the (absent) immune genetics.
